outliers.source_caption:
You want to read this caption 

It was Saturday, July 15 a week or so after Fourth of July and I poured out the last little bit of alcohol. I had in my house and decided never again.

I had a large task in front of me. I had to build a six figure company in about 30 days to be able to afford to live out here and fight for my daughter’s custody.

I had everything stripped from me. I had shut down all my other business ventures to go all in on this business with my ex-wife.

And so when she put me on the street, I essentially had to whip up a six figure business in 30 days. There was no other option she had burned the boats. It wasn’t even my choice.

And faced with that, I had two choices I could lean into vices and slow slowly wait to die

Or I could become a man let go of boyish things remove the devices from my life and become everything she told me I never could be

And I did I built the business to six figures and then seven I want custody of my daughter and I can proudly say I’m the best dad I’ve ever met

I dealt with hardships, slander and living across the country with no family or friends to help me navigate life as a simple father and a business founder at the same time

I don’t know how I did it, but I can tell you two things. It wouldn’t have happened with alcohol and it wouldn’t have happened without Jesus Christ.

Here’s to 1000 days sober

Ave Ave Christus Rex

#soberliving #sobriety #fitnesslife #businessowner #1000days

outliers.headline: The Burnout That Saved My Life
outliers.recommended_angle: Frame your transition from a burnt-out, always-on creative to a systems-driven founder as its own 'rock bottom' moment. The enemy wasn't a substance, but the hustle culture myth that promises freedom and delivers a golden cage. The victory is reclaiming your time and life.
my_hooks: It was a Tuesday in September, on a shoot in the Maldives, when I realized the dream was actually a prison.
I was sitting in an airport lounge looking at my calendar, and I couldn't find a single week off for the next year. That was the day I quit.
I had the 'perfect' creative life. The problem was, I didn't have a life at all.
Everyone saw the epic travel photos. No one saw the 18 hour days and crippling burnout behind them. This is how it changed.
I had to fire myself from the job everyone wanted, so I could finally start living.
my_talking_points: I was on a dream shoot in some remote paradise, and the only thing I could think about was how deeply exhausted I was.
I had a massive task in front of me: figure out how to keep my income without spending 300 days a year on the road, chained to a camera and an edit bay.
My entire identity was stripped down to my work. I had built this incredible career as a filmmaker, but it had completely consumed my life.
The dream I was selling to brands was one I wasn't actually living. The boats had been burned, but by my own schedule.
Faced with that reality, I had two choices. I could lean into the grind, keep chasing the next big project, and burn out for good.
Or, I could become a founder, let go of the boyish idea that constant travel meant freedom, and build something that truly served my life.
And I did. I built the systems. I used automation and AI to build an engine that runs without me. I won back my time, and I can honestly say I'm more present than I've ever been.

I don't know exactly how I pulled it off, but I can tell you two things. It wouldn't have happened if I kept clinging to my old identity, and it wouldn't have happened without obsessing over systems that buy back your time.
If you feel trapped by the business you built, we show you how to build it differently inside FieldWork.

I had built my entire identity around being a traveling filmmaker, but I had nothing left for myself. The adventure was gone. And faced with that, I had two choices. I could lean into the grind and wait for the inevitable, complete burnout. Or I could let go of that identity, remove the busywork from my life, and build a business that actually gave me freedom. And I did. I built the systems that became FieldWork. I won back my time, my relationships, and my life offline. It would not have happened if I kept clinging to my old identity, and it would not have happened without a deep focus on building systems that buy back time. That's the work we do in FieldWork.
my_script_summary: The 'dream job' was a burnout trap.
The challenge: maintain income, reclaim life.
The crossroads: continue grinding or build a new model.
The transformation: building systems to automate the work.
The victory: winning back time and freedom.
The core principle: Build Online. Live Offline.
The call to action for others feeling trapped.
production_guidance: Shoot as a simple, direct-to-camera talking head. A calm, natural setting like your office or outdoors works best.
Intersperse B-roll of old travel footage (you with a camera, on a boat, in airports) to contrast with the feeling of burnout.
Juxtapose that with new B-roll: you relaxed, walking in nature, spending time with family, not working.
Use simple on-screen text to emphasize key phrases: 'The dream was a prison', 'Systems that buy back time', and 'Build Online. Live Offline.'
End with a clear, static text card: 'Ready to build differently? Learn how in FieldWork.'

outliers.headline: Build a Prison or an Escape Plan
outliers.recommended_angle: Present founders with the two paths ahead. Path one is the default: burnout from being chained to the business. Path two is the intentional route: building systems and automation to reclaim your time and actually live the life your business is supposed to fund.
my_hooks: You can work *in* your business forever, or build one that works for you.
Founders have two choices: build a prison, or build an escape plan.
Keep trading all of your time for money, or learn how to buy it back.
Let your business own your calendar, or build one that protects your time.
Watch other people live the life you want, or build the engine that pays for it.
my_talking_points: As a founder or an expert, you really only have two choices.
The first choice is the default path. You keep trading your time for money, you answer every email, you do every task, and you become the single point of failure in your own business.
Frankly, that path is a trap. It leads to burnout, and it's why so many people build a business they secretly grow to resent.
Then there's the second path. This is where you decide to build systems. You build an engine that works for you.
You use simple automation and AI to handle the repetitive tasks that drain your energy, so you can focus on the work only you can do.
This is the difference between building yourself a high stress job and building an asset that actually serves your life.
It's about designing a business that runs efficiently, so you can get back out into the world and live.
This is the entire idea behind what we do in FieldWork. We build online so we can live offline.
If you are tired of the first path and you are ready to start building that engine, just comment the word 'engine' below and I will send you the details on how we do it.

🚨PSA to all fitness coaches 🚨

I run a coaching program I’ve taken multiple coaches to $100,000 per month Let’s make one thing crystal clear.

The coaches hitting these numbers have been in business for years

That number does not come solely from ads

It takes 6 to 12 months for those ads to double the top line revenue of the business

If those ads are going to consistently pull in that same amount of revenue month of a month

every single coach who’s doing $100,000 to $200,000 a month in our program also has amazing organic content and they post extremely consistently

Being told to do one or the other like it is a magic fix is a lie and you should know better

Cutting out carbs was never the answer so neither is cutting out organic or cutting out ads

You need a well-rounded business strategy that you stick to for 6 to 12 months

A realistic goal over there 6 to 12 months should be to double what’s your business is pulling

If you’re lucky, you may be an outlier and 3 to 4X with your Business is pulling in

do not be the clients you hate stop trying to purchase the quick fixes

If you want to see what an actual strategy looks like deployed over 6 to 12 months at minimum to 2 to 3X your business is current run rate

Then hop on a call with our team we will walk you through what this actually looks like because I am sick of seeing people give you these false promises

Heck, we won’t even ask you to buy anything. We’ll just show you exactly what the process should look like so that the next time you do decide to buy something you are armed with the knowledge of what the actual systems and timelines look like from coaches who have actually done this and stuck with it before.

And for all that is holy, please don’t go paying this man in tens of thousands of dollars to have a “$1 million client getting system install installed in the next seven days”

Like go say that to yourself in the mirror and see how stupid it sounds
outliers.why_it_worked: It validates the audience's skepticism about 'get rich quick' promises and builds trust by positioning the creator as the honest expert in a noisy market.
outliers.headline: The Truth About Automated Businesses
outliers.recommended_angle: Christian should use this direct, 'PSA' format to debunk the myth that AI and automation are instant, magic fixes. He should position FieldWork as the grounded, realistic path to building systems that create freedom, emphasizing that it takes focused work upfront to achieve the 'live offline' goal.
my_hooks: A quick PSA for every founder who wants to get their time back.
My clients build businesses so they can live offline. Let's be crystal clear about how.
Thinking AI will magically run your business for you? We need to talk.
If you're a coach or expert, this is the hard truth about building an automated business.
Stop chasing the next shiny AI tool. Here’s what actually buys back your time.
my_talking_points: This is a PSA for every founder, coach, and expert out there.
We help people in our FieldWork program build online businesses that let them live offline. But let's make one thing perfectly clear.
The people successfully doing this have spent time building foundational systems. Freedom isn't a magic button you press.
It's not about finding one perfect AI tool. That's a myth. It's about building a handful of core engines that work together.
Realistically, it takes three to six months to build and dial in these systems to a point where they are reliably buying back 10 to 20 hours of your week.
Buying a map doesn't walk the trail for you. In the same way, buying a new AI tool won't build your business for you.
You need a well rounded strategy for your marketing, sales, and fulfillment, and you need to stick with it.
So please, stop being the person who buys quick fixes. Stop chasing the shiny new tool that promises to solve everything.
If you want to see what an actual 'Build Online, Live Offline' strategy looks like, one that we deploy over several months, I am happy to show you.
I will personally walk you through the blueprint so you know what a real, grounded system looks like. No pitch, just the plan.
outliers.my_body_copy: This is a PSA for every founder, coach, and expert out there. In FieldWork, we help people build online businesses so they can truly live offline. But let’s make one thing perfectly clear. The people who are successfully getting their time back have spent time building foundational systems. Freedom is not a magic button you just press. It’s not about finding one perfect AI tool. That’s a myth. It's about building a few core engines that work together. Realistically, it takes three to six months to build these systems to a point where they reliably buy back ten to twenty hours of your week. Buying a map doesn't walk the trail for you. In the same way, a new piece of software will not build your business for you. You need a simple, grounded strategy that you stick with. So do not be the client you dislike. Stop trying to purchase quick fixes. If you want to see what an actual strategy to buy back your time looks like, I'll walk you through the blueprint myself. No pitch, just the plan.
my_script_summary: Hook: A PSA for founders who want their time back.
The Reality: Freedom comes from building systems, not finding a magic tool.
The Timeline: It takes 3 to 6 months to build engines that save you 10+ hours a week.
The Analogy: A map doesn't walk the trail for you.
The Warning: Stop chasing shiny objects and quick fixes.

production_guidance: Film as a simple, direct to camera talking head. A calm, natural setting would reinforce the 'live offline' message.
Use bold on-screen text to highlight key phrases like 'PSA FOR FOUNDERS', '3 TO 6 MONTHS', and 'BUILD AN ENGINE'.
Incorporate brief B-roll clips of you outdoors (hiking, by the water) when you mention 'live offline' to connect the concept to the visuals.

End with a clear, full-screen text CTA: 'Want the blueprint? DM me SYSTEM'.

my_hooks: How to buy back your time, speed run edition.
Speed run building a business that doesn't need you.
Want your life back from your business? Speed run it.
The founder's speed run to working less.

my_talking_points: You want to buy back your time from your business? Here’s the speed run.
Most founders don't build a business, they build a high-stress job they can't quit. They become the master bottleneck.
But there are a few key moves you can make to actually get your freedom back. It’s not easy, but it is simple.
First, you have to stop trading your time directly for money. Turn your expertise into a product or a system people can buy.
Second, build an engine that brings you clients while you sleep. Automate your lead generation and the early stages of trust building.
Third, make AI your first employee. It can handle the 80 percent of administrative work that drains your energy and focus.
Fourth, create a playbook for everything you do. If it's not written down, you're the only one who can do it, and you'll be trapped forever.
And finally, the most important step: schedule your 'offline' time first. Put it in the calendar and protect it like it's your most important client.
That’s it. That’s how you start building a business that serves your life, not the other way around.
If you want the roadmap and the tools to build these systems for yourself, that's exactly what we do inside FieldWork.

It can handle the eighty percent of admin work that drains your focus. Fourth, create a playbook for everything. If a process isn't written down, you're the only one who can do it, and you'll be trapped forever. And finally, the most important part. Schedule your 'offline' time first, and protect it like it's your most important client meeting. That’s how you build a business that serves your life. If you want the full roadmap for this, check out FieldWork.

Solution Intro: A few key strategic moves to break free.

production_guidance: On-screen text: Use a bold title like 'SPEED RUN: BUYING YOUR TIME BACK'. As you list each point, have it appear on screen as a simple numbered item.
Shot style: Simple talking head, shot in a single take. A warm, natural setting like a home office with a window or outdoors is perfect. Keep it grounded and direct.

B-roll: Briefly cut to B-roll that illustrates the 'offline' goal for each point: a shot of a digital product page, then a shot of you hiking. A shot of a system diagram, then a shot of you with family.

my_talking_points: You are building your business completely backwards, and it is not your fault.
Most of us are taught to build for more revenue, more growth, and more scale, with the hope that we can buy back our time later on.
But that is how you end up trapped, chained to a desk, running on a hamster wheel that just gets faster and demands more of you.

You don’t trust happiness.

You trust struggle.

A lot of people don’t realize they’ve spent so much time surviving that peace starts to feel suspicious.

The reading says that good feelings can actually make us anxious.

Not because something is wrong.

Because something is right.

Maybe happiness isn’t the thing leaving.

Maybe you’re just not letting it stay.

If nobody’s told you this today, I love you. I believe in you. If you want to do hard things, you can do hard things. You got this. And come back tomorrow so we can all be a little less crazy together.

I love y’all.

Peace.

outliers.why_it_worked: It uses an extremely direct and provocative hook that identifies a massive pain point. The brevity creates immediate intrigue and promises a simple solution, forcing the viewer to watch to discover what 'this' is.
outliers.headline: The Freedom Blueprint
outliers.recommended_angle: Adapt the 'If [PAIN], do this' framework for Christian's audience of founders and experts. Swap the pain of being 'broke' for the pain of being time-poor, burnt out, and trapped by the business they built, positioning FieldWork as the solution.
my_hooks: If your business owns you, do this.
If you feel trapped by your work, do this.
If you're burnt out but skilled, do this.
If you want to build online but live offline, do this.
If you're chained to your laptop, do this.
my_talking_points: If you feel trapped by the business you built, you need to hear this.
You started this company to be free, but now you work more than ever, completely chained to your laptop.
The problem isn't your skill and it is not your work ethic. The problem is the model. You are the bottleneck in your own business.
The only way out is to build systems and engines that work for you, not the other way around.
This means leveraging simple AI and automation to handle client intake, content creation, and lead generation, so you do not have to do it all manually.
Imagine your marketing running on autopilot, qualifying leads while you are on a dive or hiking with your family.
This is about building the engine once, and building it right, so your business can serve its actual purpose, which is to fund your life.
The goal is not to hustle harder. The goal is to get back out into the field and live the life you wanted when you started this whole thing.
We teach founders how to build these exact freedom engines inside my program, FieldWork.
If you are ready to build online so you can live offline, head to the link in my bio to learn how.
outliers.my_body_copy: If you feel trapped by the business you built, you need to hear this. You likely started your own thing to be free, but now you find yourself working more than ever, completely chained to your laptop. The problem is not your skill and it is definitely not your work ethic. The problem is the model. You have made yourself the bottleneck. The only way out is to build systems and engines that work for you. This means using simple AI and automation to handle things like client intake, content creation, and lead nurturing so you do not have to manually do it all. Imagine your marketing running on its own, qualifying leads while you are out on a dive or hiking with your family. This is about building the engine once, and building it right, so your business can finally serve its real purpose: to fund your life, not consume it. The goal is not to hustle harder. The goal is to get back out into the world. We teach this inside my program, FieldWork. If you are ready to build online so you can finally live offline, head to the link in my bio to learn more.
my_script_summary: Hook with the founder's core pain: feeling trapped.
Identify the flawed model: you are the bottleneck.
Introduce the solution: build automated systems and engines.
Explain the tools: simple AI and automation.
Paint the vision of the 'live offline' outcome.
State the core philosophy: build once to fund your life.
CTA for the FieldWork program.
production_guidance: Film as a simple, direct-to-camera talking head shot, chest up, in a natural setting like a home office or an outdoor space.
Use the selected hook as a bold text overlay for the first 3-5 seconds to stop the scroll.
Splice in 1-2 short, cinematic B-roll clips of 'living offline' (e.g., hiking, diving, with family) when you mention the outcome.
Keep the energy grounded and confident, speaking like you are giving honest advice to a friend.
End with a clear, simple text overlay CTA: 'FieldWork Program | Link in Bio'.

outliers.relevance_reason: Directly encourages getting rich, aligning with building financial success.
outliers.source_caption: You have a duty to get rich
outliers.why_it_worked: The original used a short, polarizing statement that challenges common beliefs about wealth and morality. This forces an immediate emotional reaction and high engagement from viewers who either strongly agree or disagree.
outliers.headline: Your Duty To Get Your Time Back
outliers.recommended_angle: Reframe the concept of 'duty' away from the hustle culture's obsession with getting rich. Instead, propose that your real duty is to build a business that frees you to live a full, present life offline with the people you love.
my_hooks: You have a duty to get your time back.
You have a duty to build a business that doesn't need you.
You have a duty to disappear for a week without checking your phone.
You have a duty to spend more time in the field than in the office.
You have a duty to live a life you don't need a vacation from.
my_talking_points: You have a duty to get your time back.
I know that sounds strange. We are told our duty is to hustle, to grind, to be 'on' 24/7 for our business.
But that’s a trap. A business that needs you every second of the day isn't an asset, it's a prison.
Your real duty is to the life you want to live. It's to your family, your health, and the experiences you're currently putting on hold for 'someday'.
The truth is, 'someday' never comes unless you build it.
You build it by creating systems, engines, and automations that handle the work for you, so you don't have to.
This is how you build a business that serves your life, not the other way around.
It's how you buy back your time, your most valuable and non renewable resource.
If you want to learn how to build the engines that get you back in the field, this is what we do in FieldWork. Check the link in my bio to learn more.
outliers.my_body_copy: You have a duty to get your time back. I know that sounds strange. We're constantly told our duty is to the hustle, to grind harder, to be available 24/7 for our business. But that whole idea is a trap. A business that requires your constant attention isn't an asset, it's a beautifully designed prison. Your real duty is to the life you actually want to live. It's a duty to your family, to your health, and to all the experiences you keep putting on hold for a 'someday' that never seems to arrive. The only way to make it arrive is to build it intentionally. You do that by creating systems, engines, and automations that do the heavy lifting for you. This is how you design a business that serves your life, not a life that serves your business. It's how you reclaim your most valuable asset: your time. If you’re ready to build the engines that get you back in the field, check out FieldWork. The link is in my bio.
my_script_summary: Hook: State the provocative duty to get your time back.
Problem: Challenge the hustle culture narrative of 'duty'.
Reframe: Your real duty is to your life, not your desk.
Solution: Build systems and automation engines.
Outcome: A business that serves your life and buys back time.
CTA: Learn how in the FieldWork program.
production_guidance: Film as a simple, direct to camera talking head, ideally in a warm, natural setting like a home office with a window or outdoors.
Use B-roll of you doing 'offline' activities: hiking, with family, by the ocean. Cut to these clips when you mention 'the life you want to live'.
On screen text: Start with the hook 'You have a duty to get your time back' on screen for the first 3 seconds.
End with a clear text overlay and a visual cue pointing to the link in your bio for FieldWork.

outliers.relevance_reason: Vaguely mentions getting 'real results for real coaches' but lacks specific connection to FieldWork's mission.
outliers.source_caption:
Two key takeaways from this…

But first an apology I am a very objective thinker and so presented with facts I drop opinions and I lean into what’s actually real

And I can’t deny he gets real results for real coaches and they’re very happy (for the most part)

I wanna see this industry grow over the next 10 years and I wanna see it grow into a place that we’re all proud to be a part of

outliers.why_it_worked: This format succeeds by combining the intrigue of controversy with the trust-building power of vulnerability. The nuanced, non-combative argument positions the creator as a mature thinker, making their ultimate point more credible and compelling.
outliers.headline: A Clarification on Hustle Culture
outliers.recommended_angle: Christian clarifies his stance on 'hustle culture,' conceding its short-term benefits while critiquing it as a long-term identity. This positions FieldWork as the strategic solution to make the 'grind' phase temporary and intentional.
my_hooks: Two key takeaways on hustle culture... but first, an apology.
I need to clarify my position on 'the grind'.
I was wrong about one thing when it comes to building a business.
Let's talk about the hustle. I have two observations, and a quick clarification.
Here's my updated take on hustle culture, and why I'm changing my tune.
my_talking_points: I have two key takeaways on 'hustle culture', but first, I need to make a clarification.
I always try to be objective, and when presented with facts, I lean into what's real. And I can't deny that intense, focused work gets real results for founders, especially in the beginning.
My first observation is this: the 'grind' is a powerful tool for a specific season. It's meant for the launch phase to build momentum, but it is not a sustainable way to run a business or a life.

Second, there is a massive difference between the results of hard work and making 'the hustle' your entire identity. I'm a huge fan of the breakthroughs that focused effort can create. I am not a fan of glorifying burnout as a lifestyle.
So I absolutely still stand by what I've always said: the goal is to build a business that serves your life, not the other way around. We build online so we can live offline.
But I don't like that in my passion for that message, I may have unfairly dismissed the role that a short, intense season of work plays. It's a necessary stage for many.
So while I stand by the end goal of getting you back out into the field, I do respect the output that a temporary, focused grind can create to get a business off the ground.

With that said, let's keep the focus on what we are all trying to do here, which is build businesses that create more freedom, not less. If you're ready to build the systems that ensure the hustle is temporary, that's exactly what we teach in FieldWork.

I always try to be an objective thinker, and when I'm presented with facts, I have to lean into what's real. It is meant for the launch phase to build momentum, but it is not a sustainable way to run a business or a life. Second, there is a huge difference between the results of hard work and making 'the hustle' your entire identity. I'm a big fan of the breakthroughs that focused effort creates. I am not a fan of glorifying burnout. So I still stand by my core belief: we build online so we can live offline. But I realize in my passion for that message, I may have unfairly dismissed the role a short, intense season of work plays. So while I stand by the mission, I respect the output a temporary grind creates. Let's focus on building businesses that create more freedom. If you're ready to build the systems that make the hustle temporary, that's what we do in FieldWork.
my_script_summary: Hook: Offer two takeaways on hustle culture, but start with a clarification.
Concession: Acknowledge that the 'grind' does produce real results, especially early on.
Nuance 1: Frame the grind as a temporary tool for a specific season, not a lifestyle.
Nuance 2: Separate the positive results of hard work from the toxic identity of 'hustling'.
Reaffirm Mission: Restate the core belief of 'Build Online. Live Offline'.
CTA: Position FieldWork as the way to build systems that make the grind phase finite.
production_guidance: Shoot as a direct-to-camera piece in a simple, grounded setting like your office or workshop. The vulnerability requires intimacy.
Use clean, minimal on-screen text to highlight 'Takeaway 1: A Tool, Not a Lifestyle' and 'Takeaway 2: Results vs. Identity'.
Incorporate B-roll that contrasts the two ideas: quick cuts of focused work on a laptop, followed by slow-motion, peaceful shots of being outdoors.
End with a clear, on-screen call to action: 'Make the grind temporary. Learn more about FieldWork in my bio.'
